Process termination at Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum suggests invalid memory access, recursion depth, or integer overflow while computing child count. Even if exact operation is unknown, any incorrect bounds checking in ASN.1 tree walking using attacker-controlled buffer is a classic memory corruption risk.
Because failure happens deep in parsing code rather than at top-level validations, it is unlikely to be purely harness-induced (JNI arguments appear reasonable: a byte array and length).
No evidence of deliberate abort on malformed input (function name is about computing children, not validate/abort); more likely mis-parses malformed input, leading to crash.

Given: custom ASN.1/X.509 implementation in security-critical context, fuzz-induced crash in internal tree computation, and attacker-controlled certificate input path, classify as likely memory-safety vulnerability of at least DoS, possibly exploitable.
Cannot assign specific CWE with certainty but out-of-bounds read/write (CWE-787/125) or improper input validation (CWE-20) are most plausible.

chain_of_thought: Tools are failing (404), so I cannot inspect the binary or Java; I must reason only from the provided stack and symbols.
Top native frame is __memcpy, called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST inside libUbikeyJni.so while parsing DER ASN.1 leading to X.509 cert conversion and CERT_Load, then JNI jGetPolicyOID.
A memcpy crash in ASN.1/DER decode logic is very often due to using lengths from the encoded data (attacker-controlled) without sufficient bounds checks, causing out-of-bounds read/write or overlap.

The call chain Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> Get_DER_Child -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> CERT_Load strongly suggests parsing arbitrary DER/X.509 blobs, and the fuzzer presumably feeds untrusted ASN.1 into this path through jGetPolicyOID.

JNI bridge jGetPolicyOID likely accepts a byte array / buffer from Java; typical design is that Java length is used as input length; fuzz harness probably mimics that, so the bug is not purely harness-only.

Because the process termination is in __memcpy rather than a deliberate abort, and no sanitizer frame indicated, this is consistent with an actual memory safety bug (CWE-787/119) inside DER decode that fails to validate lengths from the input before memcpy.

I cannot prove the exact line, but the combination of function names, memcpy crash, and fuzzing context gives high likelihood this is a genuine, reachable memory corruption on malformed certificate data.

Impact: if this JNI is usable on attacker-supplied certs (e.g., smart card, server cert, file), a remote or local attacker could cause app crash and potentially exploit heap corruption; conservatively rate as high severity.
